key_insight: In rough, load-bearing contacts, two-component lubrication outperforms water and dodecane by maintaining plug flow to lower sliding speeds while reducing friction and material transfer.
engineering_relevance: The results suggest that fluid pairing can be used to improve boundary/mixed-lubrication performance in real rough interfaces without relying on polymer brushes.
method: Non-equilibrium molecular dynamics of sliding between rough, deformable surfaces
Compared single-component water and n-dodecane against an immiscible two-fluid lubricant
Evaluated friction, load-bearing behavior, plug flow, cavitation, and wear/transfer under identical geometry
evidence_signal: The abstract reports comparative simulation results showing stronger speed dependence and lower load-bearing capacity for water than dodecane, lowest friction and transfer for the mixed lubricant, and cavitation-driven stress release in water.
watch_item: The favorable mixed-lubricant behavior was demonstrated in molecular dynamics under specific rough-contact geometries, so transfer to real devices will depend on scale, surface chemistry, and operating conditions.
paper_title: Plug Flow and Cavitation in Rough Lubricated Contacts: Molecular Dynamics of Single- vs. Two-Component Fluids
